subject: Corporate Fears As Pride Month Nears | Most Valuable Soccer Teams | Buffett Wisdom
body: Forbes CxO Newsletter Plus: Japan’s 50 Richest ADVERTISEMENT Corporate Fears As Pride Month Is Here The plot line sounds almost too scary to be a classic Disney tale: A leader works for years to help create a community and company that fully embraces those who identify as lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender or queer (LGBTQ+), only to find themselves cast as a villain in a culture war. Rainbow-colored merchandise suddenly become targets of instead of cheerful reminders that everyone should feel free to choose who they love and how they live. Important debates about gender-affirming care for minors or the impact of transgender athletes in competition can’t happen as there’s no middle where people can meet.And so Target pulls some of its Pride merchandise from stores and displays because of a backlash in one corner, sparking a backlash in another. Bud Light enlists transgender influencer Dylan Mulvaney for a social media campaign, only to backtrack and distance itself when that results in calls for a boycott. And lest we assume boycotts are the tool of choice for only Republicans, there’s Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom, who tweeted that the state would cut ties with Walgreens after it announced it would not distribute abortion pills in other states where attorneys general have told it not to. Newsom backed down.And then, of course, there’s Disney. Bob Iger stepped down as CEO in early 2020 after a successful 15-year run, only to return to the role 20 months later as the entertainment and media giant had faltered on several fronts--most notably through a high-stakes feud with Florida Governor Ron DeSantis as he was about to sign what’s been called the ‘Don’t Say Gay’ bill into law. While then-CEO Bob Chapek initially tried to steer clear of the controversy as it was about to be passed, Iger tweeted that the law would put “young LGBTQ people in jeopardy.” Desantis stripped Disney of its decades-old tax status and self-governing privileges; Iger then canceled plans for a $1 billion office complex earlier this month, prompting a defiant response from DeSantis. This kaiju-like battle of the behemoths has moved to the courts, where it’s proving to be bloody for both sides. The conflict almost overshadowed the governor’s official announcement last week that he’s running for president. Meanwhile, new polls suggest consumers increasingly view Disney as a polarizing brand, which is rarely great for business.It's also an unwelcome distraction as Iger is trying to cut 7,000 jobs and billions in costs while trying to stanch the outflow of Disney+ subscribers and reinvigorate its film franchise with bets like a live-action remake of The Little Mermaid. [Check out Suzanne Kelleher’s great piece on the high-flying death of Disney’s $2,500-a-night Star Wars Hotel.]With the wrath of extremist consumers moving on to Kohl's and other brands, what's a business leader to do? Stress inclusivity as a core value. Don't play the culture wars as a marketing tactic to drive sales. (I'm talking to you, M&Ms) And link those values to wider commitments to the community, from financial inclusion (Here's an interview with John Hope Bryant) to employee benefits. If your support for LGBTQ+ rights only comes out once a year in the form of multicolored products that you'll put away at the first sign of protest, perhaps this is a good month to reflect on the culture you need to build. It’s Hard To Rave About A Plain White T-Shirt, So Retailers Are Making AI Do It: Some retailers are using AI to churn out hundreds of product descriptions that used to take weeks to write.Has Twitter lost 2/3 of its value under Elon Musk? subject: Morning Distribution for Tuesday, May 23, 2023
body: A email Morning Distribution Tuesday, May 23, 2023 Your daily briefing from FiveThirtyEight The Morning Story Tim Scott’s Uphill Battle To Win The Republican Nomination By Alex Samuels A decisive Election Day win last year. A prime speaking slot at a national Republican convention. That elusive “fresh face” that a plurality of GOP voters have told pollsters they want going into next year’s election. If you think I’m talking about Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis, you’re mistaken. Yes, he checks these boxes, too. And yes, these are objectively good résumé boosters to have if you want to seek — let alone win — the Republican nomination for president. But there’s another name to watch who embodies these same traits: South Carolina Sen. Tim Scott, now the fifth major Republican candidate (after former President Donald Trump, former South Carolina Gov. Nikki Haley, entrepreneur Vivek Ramaswamy and former Arkansas Gov. Asa Hutchinson) to announce their candidacy for president. Though it’s not particularly common for two candidates from the same state to seek the presidency, there are plenty of reasons for Scott to run. He’s a rising star within the party — given both a marquee speaking slot at the 2020 Republican National Convention and the opportunity to deliver the Republican response to President Biden’s State of the Union in 2021 — who enjoys relative popularity in his own state and is sitting on a campaign war chest of over $21 million. And if Republicans want to build a more diverse voting base, Scott could have broader appeal to voters as a Black man. subject: Re: sa-learn --ham ground rules
body: On Fri, 2008-02-08 at 01:49 -0500, Gene Heskett wrote: > The sa-learn --spam can process a message in 5 to 10 seconds or so, so if I've > dropped 20 doofus mails in the spam directory and fire it off, I have it done > and kmail is back among the living in 2-3 minutes. This seems *way* too high. If there have been only 20 messages total in that folder, sa-learn should have processed these in a few *seconds* or less. > But, feeding it a 'ham' directory with about 7k messages in it, turned > sa-learn into a 100% cpu hog, [...] What did you expect? Based on your numbers above, processing that folder would have taken 10-20 *hours*... > incrementing the message processed number only > about every 3 to 5 minutes. I couldn't kill it, it kept coming back and I > must have fed it a kill -9 50 times. Hmm. Kmail doesn't start one process per mail by any chance? > So what is the maximum number of files in a directory that one can feed to > sa-learn --ham and expect it to achieve normal speed? Dunno if there are limitations -- however, your 7k messages should be perfectly fine. Just ran a test on a 6k messages mbox file, and there was no noticeable difference to a 30 messages test. > The command that kmail issues to it is: > sa-learn --ham /root/Mail/(foldername)/cur You're not using root as your ordinary user account, do you !? guenther -- char *t="\\10pse\\0r\\0dtu\\0.@ghno\\x4e\\xc8\\x79\\xf4\\xab\\x51\\x8a\\x10\\xf4\\xf4\\xc4"; main(){ char h,m=h=*t++,*x=t+2*h,c,i,l=*x,s=0; for (i=0;i>=1)||!t[s+h]){ putchar(t[s]);h=m;s=0; }}}

Informasjon til deg som er tillitsvalgt i Pensjonistforbundet Takk for den gode jobben du gjør!
Vi er nå litt over tre aktive måneder inn i 2025, og kan ta en velfortjent pust i bakken. Jeg vil gjerne takke hver især for den viktig og gode jobben dere gjør, rundt om i hele landet.Pensjonistforbundet jobber for aldersvennlige lokalsamfunn, der alle kan leve gode liv. Vi lager sosiale møteplasser for å motvirke ensomhet. Vi jobber politisk, for å sikre økonomisk trygghet og velferd for alle, og helsetjenester når vi trenger det. Pluss mye mer.
La det ikke være noen tvil: Sammen gjør vi samfunnet bedre! Og når vi tar fatt igjen etter påskedagene, må vi jobbe videre for å invitere flere inn i fellesskapet vårt, slik at vi får enda bedre uttelling for den gode jobben vi gjør. Det er vårt felles mål.Med ønske om en god påske,Jan Davidsenforbundsleder
Tips til aktivitet: Bocciaturnering for ung og gammel!
Pensjonistforbundet Hemsedal inviterer unge og gamle til bocciaturnering hver 2. mandag i måneden. Boccia er en sport for alle, uansett alder eller erfaring, og denne aktiviteten skaper engasjement!🎉
Bordene dekkes med kaffe og noe godt å bite i, og praten går livlig før turneringen starter. Noen kommer for å vinne, andre for å prøve noe nytt, men alle har én ting til felles: gleden over å være sammen. Her er det jubelbrøl når noen treffer blink, og latteren sitter løst når et og annet kast går litt skeis. Spillet er preget av god sportsånd og vennskapelig konkurranse. 👏
Til sist blir det kåret en vinner, men disse kveldene handler om mer enn bare resultater. Dette er en påminnelse for oss alle om hvor viktig det er å møtes, være aktive og ha det gøy sammen.🥰
Flere tips til aktiviteter finner du her
Nytt i nettbutikken: Caps og brosjyre

Nå finner du fine caps og en flunkende ny brosjyre for medlemsfordelene i Pensjonistforbundets nettbutikk. 
Dette gir økt synlighet og nyttig informasjon i det viktige arbeidet med å verve flere medlemmer.Lykke til! 👍
Lenke til nettbutikken
Medlemsservice er stengt fra og med mandag 14.4 til og med mandag 21.4. Sender du epost, vil den bli besvart fra og med tirsdag 22.4. Hold deg oppdatert på saker som angår deg, følg oss på Facebook og Instagram!

body: First Up Plus: Why are so many condo elevators broken? ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ LIVING HERE By Amber Shortt Good afternoon. I’m Amber Shortt, the Star’s housing editor. Here’s the latest on broken condo elevators, young investors turning to real estate and when to list your house. GOOD WEEK FOR... Housing advocates. A new report says Toronto’s post-pandemic oversupply of office space could last for decades, bolstering arguments to turn the space toward residential buildings. Recent homebuyers. Long maligned, the mortgage stress test is getting credit for blunting pain among new owners as rates jumped 4.25 percentage points over the space of 15 months. Mortgage brokers. Many fixed-rate fans are opting for a three-year term over the slightly lower five-year fixed rate in anticipation of possible lower rates next year. Where should you eat next? If you’re wondering where to eat as the city heats up, you need Food Crawl, the Star’s free food newsletter. Whether it’s a strip mall standout or a downtown gem, Food Crawl will make you an insider on the best eats in Toronto and beyond. Sign up for free here. BAD WEEK FOR... The city. Toronto is suing developers for allegedly failing to repay the $200,000 cost of protecting a shelter from neighbouring condo construction — claims the developers deny. This landlord. A man was sentenced to 20 years in prison for allowing a tenant to set up an opioid drug lab in the basement of his Pickering rental home, while a family unwittingly rented upstairs. Residents on the 40th floor. The near monopoly of elevator installation and repair in Toronto means plenty of condo elevators are out of service. Here’s what the regulator’s doing. TRENDING It used to be that when young people graduated school, they were consumed with finding that first job. Now, increasingly, they’re looking to nab that first investment property, too. A recent survey for Royal LePage found more than a quarter of Canadians who had yet to jump into the investment market planned to do so within the next five years, with about half of them between the ages of 18 and 34. And they’re willing to couch surf to do it. The survey found 33 per cent of younger investors bought their first income property before a home to live in themselves, either while renting or living with family. They’re also the most likely of any cohort to buy more than one income property, often pairing with a financing partner, be it family, friend or other, to afford it. And who can blame them? Considering, for example, the average cancer researcher makes roughly $57,000 a year — just over half the required $100,000 income to comfortably lease a one-bedroom apartment in Toronto, and barely enough to meet the $55,000 salary to uncomfortably rent one — it wouldn’t be surprising if today’s youth see real estate as more lucrative than their day job. It's a notion that was floated by Ben Rabidoux, president of market research firm North Cove Advisors, back in 2021 at the height of the pandemic market when speculation purchases were running rampant. “I don’t know how many people who would have ended up in the STEM field are instead flipping houses. It’s so lucrative right now you can’t blame them, but I don’t know if, from a longer-term perspective, that enhances productivity in our country,” he told the Star at the time. It seems Gen Z and millennial investors are just taking a page out of the boomer playbook. Those aged 55 and older were vastly overrepresented among real estate investors in Ontario, making up 57 per cent despite being just 32 per cent of the population, a recent Statistics Canada report showed. For them, the investment has paid off — whether that will repeat for today’s young investors remains to be seen. “If you go back over 65 years, the real estate market continues to outperform many of the other indices — the TSX or the NASDAQ,” Mike Heddle of Royal LePage State Realty told the Star’s Tess Kalinowski. But another report, this time from CIBC and Urbanation, warns that many investors who bought pre-construction condos just a few years ago are now underwater, as rising interest rates and costs have put half of condo investments in the red. Still, after watching everyone from firefighters to chefs to nurses not be able to afford to both work and live in Toronto, it seems the city’s blistering housing market may have turned the old adage of “don’t quit your day job” for your side project on its head. Now, if you want a day job, be it teaching high school or curing cancer, the advice might be “don’t quit your realtor.” LOCATION, LOCATION,LOCATION NEWS FROM AROUND THE GTA AND BEYOND For those who want to get away from it all while still having it all close by, this $700,000 Wasaga cottage is a five-minute drive from a Walmart. Check it out. Residents along Eglinton Avenue face seemingly endless construction — and they live on the second-worst road in Ontario. These other roads made the list. Can’t afford a house in Toronto? Maybe you can buy a castle in Monaco — or any of these other glamorous properties cheaper than local real estate. Here’s what the creators of the new book Denison Avenue have to say about the history and current state of Toronto’s Chinatown and Kensington Market. Japanese researchers made a house made partially of shredded disposable diapers. Here’s why this breakthrough is the s---. [Science News] Function doesn’t trump form in this 10,000-square-foot King home, where all you see is “the really pretty things.” Look inside. SHOULD I LIST MY HOME NOW OR WAIT TILL THE FALL? ADVICE FROM THE EXPERTS With prices rising but the traditionally slow days of summer real estate on their way, the Star talked to One Group broker Nasma Ali about whether it’s best to list a home now or wait until the fall market. Here’s what she said: Whether you decide to sell now or wait until fall depends on your circumstances, and there’s advantages to both. If you have already purchased a home to move into, or a tenant has moved out and you'd like to avoid vacancy months, then selling now is best. In the summer, families with children are also trying to buy and settle down before the start of the new school year. However, summer activity becomes exceptionally slow in August — specifically the last half of August when everyone is scrambling to enjoy the last days of sunshine and vacation. If you get to August and you have time to wait, then it may be worth waiting until after Labour Day. My advice would be to be patient and list the second week of September, as there tends to be a flood of listings that first week. Waiting lets you observe any new sale benchmarks or records set and price accordingly. The fall market offers you a small window of prime activity, until around mid October. After that, activity becomes more steady. Have a burning real estate question of your own? Email us at livinghere@thestar.ca. Thanks for reading. Living Here will be back in your inbox next week.
